expanded_description: Understanding text structure is the skill of recognizing how an author has organized a passage—the underlying architecture that determines how ideas are sequenced, how arguments are built, and how information flows from introduction to conclusion. On the SAT, text structure questions ask students to identify the organizational pattern of a passage, to understand why an author placed a particular piece of information at a particular point, and to predict what kind of content is likely to follow given the structural logic established. Unlike main idea questions that ask what the passage says, structure questions ask how the passage works—the rhetorical and logical moves the author makes to achieve their purpose. Mastery of text structure enables students to read more efficiently, to locate information quickly, and to understand the function of each part of a passage within the whole.

core_concept.principle:
Text structure is the organizational framework that governs the sequence and relationship of ideas in a passage. Every passage has a structure, whether carefully planned or intuitively developed, and that structure shapes how the reader processes the information. Recognizing the structure allows the reader to anticipate what comes next, to understand why certain information appears where it does, and to see the logical relationships between different parts of the argument.

The most common organizational patterns on the SAT can be grouped into six broad categories, each with characteristic signal words and logical relationships:

(1) Thesis-Evidence / Claim-Support Structure: The passage opens with a central claim or thesis, then supports it with evidence, examples, reasoning, or data. This is the most common structure for argumentative and informational passages. Signal words: 'for example,' 'for instance,' 'studies show,' 'the evidence indicates,' 'furthermore,' 'additionally.'

(2) Problem-Solution Structure: The passage identifies a problem, challenge, or question, then proposes one or more solutions or answers, often evaluating the merits of each. Signal words: 'the challenge,' 'one proposed solution,' 'an alternative approach,' 'to address this,' 'the problem can be solved by.'

(3) Cause-Effect Structure: The passage describes a cause (or multiple causes) and traces its effects, or describes an effect and traces its causes back. Signal words: 'as a result,' 'consequently,' 'led to,' 'was caused by,' 'the reason for,' 'this had the effect of.'

(4) Compare-Contrast Structure: The passage examines similarities and differences between two or more subjects, ideas, or approaches. Signal words: 'similarly,' 'likewise,' 'in contrast,' 'however,' 'on the other hand,' 'differs from,' 'both share.'

(5) Chronological / Sequential Structure: The passage organizes information in temporal order, tracing developments over time or presenting a sequence of steps. Signal words: 'first,' 'then,' 'subsequently,' 'later,' 'finally,' 'after,' 'before,' 'in the following years.'

(6) Question-Answer / Hypothesis-Testing Structure: The passage poses a research question or hypothesis, describes the investigation or reasoning used to test it, and presents the results or conclusion. Common in science passages. Signal words: 'the question remains,' 'to test this hypothesis,' 'researchers designed,' 'the results showed,' 'this finding suggests.'
core_concept.explanation:
Think of text structure as the blueprint of a building. If you understand the blueprint, you can navigate the building efficiently—you know where the load-bearing walls (the main arguments) are, how the rooms (paragraphs) connect to each other, and what purpose each space serves. If you do not understand the blueprint, you wander through the building noting individual rooms and features but never quite grasping how they fit together. A reader who understands text structure reads with a sense of direction and purpose, always aware of where they are in the argument and where the author is likely to go next.

Consider a passage that begins: 'The collapse of the Mayan civilization has long puzzled archaeologists. Several competing theories have been proposed to explain this dramatic decline.' This is a clear Problem-Solution opening: the problem is the unexplained collapse, and the reader can anticipate that the passage will present and evaluate various proposed explanations. Recognizing this structure immediately gives the reader a framework for organizing the information that follows—each theory will be a 'solution' to the 'problem,' and the author will likely evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of each before reaching a conclusion about which is most convincing.

On the SAT, structure questions take several forms. Some ask directly: 'Which of the following best describes the overall organization of the passage?' Others ask about the function of a specific paragraph: 'The third paragraph primarily serves to...' Others ask about transitions: 'The author uses the word

in line X primarily to signal...' Still others ask about what a passage has just done or is about to do: 'In the preceding paragraph, the author has most likely...' or 'Which of the following would most logically follow the final paragraph?' All of these questions are answered more easily when the reader has constructed a mental map of the passage's structure.

image.alt: Six panels arranged in two rows of three, each illustrating one of the six common text structures: Thesis-Evidence (claim with supporting arrows), Problem-Solution (problem box with solution arrows), Cause-Effect (causal chain), Compare-Contrast (Venn diagram), Chronological (timeline), and Question-Answer (question mark with arrow to answer). Each panel has signal words listed beneath it.
image.caption: The six common text structures on the SAT, each with its own logical organization and characteristic signal words that guide the reader through the passage

sections.section_title: Foundation: Mapping the Six Common Text Structures
sections.introduction: Before students can answer structure questions on the SAT, they must develop fluency in recognizing the six common organizational patterns and understanding the logical relationships each pattern establishes between ideas. This section defines each structure in detail, provides examples, and teaches the signal words that help identify the structure in a passage.

key_concept.definition:
Each of the six common text structures establishes a distinct logical relationship between the ideas in a passage. The structure determines how information is sequenced and how different parts of the passage relate to each other and to the whole. Recognizing the structure is a matter of identifying the overarching logical framework—not just spotting a signal word here or there, but understanding the overall architecture that organizes the passage's content.

The six structures are not always mutually exclusive; a passage may employ multiple structures in combination. A science passage might have an overall Question-Answer structure, with a Methods section that is Chronological (steps of the experiment) and a Discussion section that is Compare-Contrast (the study's findings compared to previous research). The skilled reader identifies the dominant structure while recognizing the subordinate structures within it.

A useful diagnostic question for identifying a passage's structure is: 'What large-scale logical move does this passage make from beginning to end?' Does it move from claim to evidence? From problem to solution? From cause to effect? From comparison to conclusion? From question to answer? The answer to this question reveals the primary structure.
key_concept.context: SAT structure questions typically appear as the first or second question for a passage, testing whether the student has grasped the overall organization before diving into detail questions. The ability to answer the structure question quickly and accurately sets the student up for success on the remaining questions by providing a framework for understanding the passage.

categories.description: The passage presents a central claim and then supports it through various forms of evidence.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: The passage opens with a clear thesis or claim, often in the first or second paragraph. Subsequent paragraphs each provide a distinct piece of evidence, a different line of reasoning, or a different example that supports the central claim. The conclusion often restates the thesis with greater force.

Example structure: Paragraph 1: Thesis stated—'The widespread adoption of smartphones has fundamentally altered adolescent social development.' Paragraph 2: Evidence from studies on screen time and face-to-face interaction. Paragraph 3: Evidence on social media and anxiety. Paragraph 4: Evidence on sleep disruption. Paragraph 5: Restated thesis—the changes are profound and lasting.

SAT question: 'The second paragraph (lines X-Y) primarily serves to...' The answer will be some version of 'provide evidence supporting the claim made in the first paragraph.' The function of each body paragraph is to support the thesis.

categories.description: The passage identifies a significant problem and then proposes, evaluates, or advocates for solutions.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: The opening defines a problem—its nature, scope, causes, or consequences. The passage then pivots to one or more proposed solutions, evaluating each. Signal words: 'challenge,' 'crisis,' 'dilemma,' 'to address this,' 'one approach,' 'an alternative,' 'proponents argue,' 'critics contend.'

Example structure: Paragraph 1: The problem of declining bee populations and its consequences for agriculture is described. Paragraph 2: One proposed solution—restricting neonicotinoid pesticides—is presented with its supporting evidence. Paragraph 3: A second proposed solution—creating pollinator corridors—is presented. Paragraph 4: The author evaluates both solutions and argues for a combined approach.

SAT question: 'Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?' Answer: 'A problem is identified, two proposed solutions are evaluated, and a combined approach is recommended.' The structural description follows the logical arc: problem, solutions, evaluation, recommendation.

categories.description: The passage traces causal relationships, either from cause to effect or from effect back to cause.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: The passage is organized around explaining why something happened or what consequences resulted from something. Signal words: 'led to,' 'resulted in,' 'was caused by,' 'contributed to,' 'had the effect of,' 'the reasons for,' 'as a consequence.'

Important distinction: In a Cause-Effect passage, the author is making causal claims. In a science passage describing a study, the Results section may be descriptive (what happened) while the Discussion section is causal (why it happened). The student must recognize when the structure shifts from description to causal explanation.

Example structure: Paragraph 1: The Dust Bowl of the 1930s is introduced as a major environmental and social catastrophe. Paragraph 2: The immediate cause—severe drought—is described. Paragraph 3: The underlying causes—poor agricultural practices, removal of native grasses, economic pressures—are analyzed. Paragraph 4: The effects—mass migration, economic devastation, changes in federal agricultural policy—are traced. Paragraph 5: The long-term legacy for American farming is considered.

categories.description: The passage examines similarities and differences between two or more subjects.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: The passage is organized around the relationship between two or more things—ideas, theories, historical periods, scientific findings, characters. Signal words for comparison: 'similarly,' 'likewise,' 'in the same way,' 'both,' 'share,' 'parallel.' Signal words for contrast: 'however,' 'in contrast,' 'on the other hand,' 'differs from,' 'unlike,' 'whereas,' 'conversely.'

Two common variants: (1) Point-by-point comparison: The passage alternates between the subjects, comparing them on one dimension at a time. (2) Block comparison: The passage discusses one subject fully, then discusses the other fully, then draws conclusions about their relationship.

Example structure: A passage comparing two economic theories. Paragraph 1: Introduces both theories and the debate between them. Paragraph 2: Theory A's explanation of market behavior. Paragraph 3: Theory B's contrasting explanation. Paragraph 4: Strengths and weaknesses of each. Paragraph 5: The author argues for a synthesis of the two approaches.

SAT question: 'The author discusses Theory A primarily to...' Answer: 'provide a contrast with Theory B that highlights the distinctive features of each.'

categories.description: The passage presents events or steps in temporal or procedural order.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: The passage is organized by time—what happened first, next, later. Or it is organized by process—step one, step two, step three. Signal words: 'first,' 'initially,' 'then,' 'subsequently,' 'later,' 'finally,' 'after,' 'before,' 'in the following decade,' 'by the time.'

Chronological structure is common in historical narratives, biographical passages, and descriptions of how scientific discoveries unfolded. Sequential structure is common in passages that explain a process or mechanism.

Example structure (historical): The passage traces the development of the civil rights movement. Paragraph 1: Early activism in the 1940s and the legal strategy. Paragraph 2: The Montgomery bus boycott and the emergence of Martin Luther King Jr. Paragraph 3: The sit-ins and freedom rides of the early 1960s. Paragraph 4: The March on Washington and the legislative victories of 1964-65. Paragraph 5: The shift toward Black Power and the legacy of the movement.

categories.description: The passage poses a research question or hypothesis and then presents the investigation that addresses it.

examples.explanation:
Identifying features: Common in science passages. The passage opens with a research question, a puzzle, or a hypothesis. It then describes the study, experiment, or investigation designed to address it. It concludes with the results and their implications. Signal words: 'researchers have long wondered,' 'the question remains,' 'to test this hypothesis,' 'the study was designed to,' 'the results revealed,' 'these findings suggest.'

This structure closely mirrors the scientific method: question, hypothesis, method, results, interpretation. The layers of evidence discussed in Lesson 2.8 (Evidence in Science Passages) map directly onto this structure.

Example structure: Paragraph 1: Research question—Does meditation affect brain structure? Paragraph 2: Hypothesis—Regular meditation practice increases gray matter density in regions associated with attention and emotional regulation. Paragraph 3: Methods—An eight-week study with MRI scans before and after. Paragraph 4: Results—Significant increases in gray matter density were observed. Paragraph 5: Interpretation—Meditation appears to produce structural brain changes, with implications for mental health treatment.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Structure from a Passage
worked_examples.problem:
Read the following passage and determine its primary organizational structure. Then evaluate the candidate structural descriptions.

Passage: 'For much of the twentieth century, the dominant view among economists was that free trade invariably benefits all participating nations. According to this theory, each country specializes in producing what it produces most efficiently, and the resulting exchange creates overall gains that outweigh any localized losses. In the 1990s, however, a growing number of economists began to challenge this consensus. They pointed to the experience of manufacturing communities in developed nations, where the loss of jobs to overseas competition had created persistent unemployment and social disruption. The new research argued that while free trade does create aggregate gains, the distribution of those gains is highly uneven, and the costs borne by displaced workers can be severe and long-lasting. More recent scholarship has sought to reconcile these two perspectives, acknowledging the overall benefits of trade while emphasizing the need for policies—such as retraining programs, wage insurance, and place-based investment—to compensate those who lose out. This synthesis represents the emerging consensus in the field: trade is beneficial, but its benefits must be actively shared to be socially sustainable.'

step_by_step.step: Step 1: Trace the Logical Arc of the Passage
step_by_step.explanation: Paragraph 1: Presents the original view (free trade always benefits all). Paragraph 2: Presents the challenge to that view (benefits are uneven; costs are concentrated). Paragraph 3: Presents the emerging synthesis (trade is beneficial but requires compensatory policies). The passage moves from one view, to a contrasting view, to a synthesis that reconciles them. This arc is the key to identifying the structure.

Candidate A: 'A problem is identified, and a solution is proposed.' The passage does not identify a 'problem' in the Problem-Solution sense. The original consensus is not a 'problem,' and the synthesis is not a 'solution' to a problem. The arc is about intellectual debate, not practical problem-solving. Eliminate.

Candidate B: 'A prevailing theory is presented, a challenge to that theory is described, and a synthesis that incorporates elements of both is offered.' This precisely captures the logical arc: original consensus, challenge, synthesis. This is a variant of Compare-Contrast structure applied to intellectual history—the passage compares and evaluates competing views before arriving at a resolution. Keep.

Candidate C: 'Events are narrated in chronological order from the twentieth century to the present.' While the passage uses temporal markers ('twentieth century,' 'In the 1990s,' 'More recent'), it is not primarily a chronological narrative. The temporal markers simply locate the intellectual positions in time. The primary logic is not 'this happened, then this happened,' but 'this view, then this contrasting view, then this synthesis.' The structure is argumentative, not narrative. Eliminate.

Candidate D: 'A hypothesis is tested, and the results are reported.' This is the Question-Answer / Hypothesis-Testing structure, typical of science passages. The passage does not present a hypothesis, an experiment, or results. It traces an intellectual debate. Eliminate.

Correct: Candidate B.

worked_examples.final_answer: The primary organizational structure is the presentation of a prevailing theory, followed by a challenge, and concluding with a synthesis. This is a compare-contrast structure applied to the history of economic thought, where the comparison ultimately yields a resolution. The passage does not simply list ideas; it traces a debate and its emerging resolution.
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example demonstrates that identifying structure requires looking past surface features (like temporal markers) to the underlying logical relationships. The passage's chronological language might tempt a student to select 'chronological,' but the deeper logic—thesis, antithesis, synthesis—is the true organizing principle.

image.alt: A decision flowchart for identifying text structure: Read the passage -> Ask: What is the large-scale logical move? -> Six branches for the six structures, each with diagnostic questions and example answer stems.
image.caption: Identifying text structure requires asking about the large-scale logical move the passage makes from beginning to end

sections.introduction: This section provides practice with the specific formats of SAT text structure questions. Passages from multiple genres are accompanied by questions that test the ability to identify overall organization, understand the function of specific paragraphs, and predict logical continuations.

key_concept.definition:
SAT structure questions test multiple levels of structural awareness: the overall organization of the passage, the function of a specific paragraph or section within that organization, the role of a transitional word or phrase in signaling structural relationships, and the logical relationship between adjacent paragraphs. A strong understanding of the passage's structure provides the framework for answering all of these question types.

Structure questions often use language like: 'The primary purpose of the second paragraph is to...' 'Which of the following best describes the relationship between the third and fourth paragraphs?' 'The author uses the phrase
to signal a transition from...' 'Which of the following would most logically follow the final sentence of the passage?'
key_concept.context: Structure awareness is not only directly tested; it also supports performance on all other question types. Knowing where the author states the thesis, where the evidence is, where the counterargument is addressed, and where the conclusion begins allows the student to locate information quickly for detail and evidence questions.

categories.type: Practice: Overall Structure and Paragraph Function
categories.description: A passage followed by questions about overall organization and the function of specific paragraphs.

examples.explanation:
Passage: '[1] The invention of the mechanical clock in medieval Europe is often cited as a pivotal moment in the history of technology, but its most profound effects were cultural rather than technical. [2] Before the mechanical clock, time was measured by natural rhythms—the rising and setting of the sun, the changing of seasons, the canonical hours of the monastery. [3] Time was fluid, approximate, and embedded in the texture of daily life. [4] The mechanical clock, by contrast, introduced a new kind of time: abstract, precise, and divorced from natural cycles. [5] Hours became identical units, interchangeable and measurable. [6] This transformation had far-reaching consequences. [7] It made possible the coordination of complex activities—markets, transportation schedules, shift work—that were essential to industrialization. [8] It also, as historian Lewis Mumford argued, reshaped human consciousness itself, training people to think of time as a commodity that could be spent, saved, or wasted. [9] In this sense, the clock did not merely measure time; it created a new experience of temporality that would come to define modern life.' Question 1: Which of the following best describes the overall organization of the passage?
A) A chronological narrative of the development of timekeeping technology.
B) A problem-solution analysis of the challenges of medieval timekeeping.
C) A claim about the cultural significance of an invention, followed by explanation and elaboration of that claim.
D) A comparison of medieval and modern attitudes toward punctuality.

Question 2: The function of sentences 2-4 ('Before the mechanical clock... natural cycles') is to:
A) Provide evidence for the claim that the mechanical clock was technically innovative.
B) Establish a contrast between pre-clock and post-clock experiences of time that supports the passage's central argument.
C) Argue that medieval life was disorganized and inefficient before the invention of the clock.
D) Present a counterargument that the passage will then refute.

The passage opens with a clear thesis: the clock's 'most profound effects were cultural rather than technical.' It then elaborates this thesis by contrasting pre-clock time (natural, fluid) with post-clock time (abstract, precise) and tracing the consequences. This is a Thesis-Evidence structure, where the evidence is an explanatory contrast and an analysis of consequences. Choice C captures this: 'A claim about the cultural significance of an invention, followed by explanation and elaboration of that claim.' Choice A (chronological narrative) is incorrect because the passage is organized around a thesis, not a timeline. Choice B (problem-solution) is incorrect because no problem is identified. Choice D (comparison of attitudes) is too narrow—the comparison between pre- and post-clock time serves the larger thesis about cultural significance.

Sentences 2-4 describe time before the clock and then contrast it with time after the clock. This contrast is the foundation for the passage's argument that the clock changed the experience of time. Choice B correctly identifies the function: establishing a contrast that supports the central argument. Choice A is incorrect because the function is not about technical innovation—the passage explicitly prioritizes cultural effects. Choice C is incorrect because the passage does not judge pre-clock life as 'disorganized and inefficient'; it describes it as different. Choice D is incorrect because the contrast is the author's own argument, not a counterargument to be refuted.

categories.description: A passage excerpt followed by a question about what would most logically come next.

examples.explanation:
Passage: 'The gut microbiome has been linked to an astonishing array of health outcomes, from metabolic disorders to mental health. As research in this field has matured, however, a note of caution has begun to sound. Many of the most widely cited studies linking specific gut bacteria to specific diseases have been correlational, and the causal mechanisms they propose remain largely untested. Moreover, the commercial probiotics industry has been quick to capitalize on preliminary findings, marketing supplements with claims that far outstrip what the science can currently support.'

Question: Which of the following would most logically follow the final sentence of the passage?

A) A detailed description of the specific bacterial strains that have been linked to improved mental health outcomes.
B) A call for stricter regulation of probiotic marketing and for more rigorous clinical trials to establish causal mechanisms.
C) A historical overview of how the gut microbiome was first discovered and how research into it has developed over time.
D) A series of case studies of patients whose health improved after taking probiotic supplements.

The passage establishes a note of caution: studies are correlational, causal mechanisms are untested, and industry claims outstrip the science. The logical continuation would follow from this critical stance. Choice B does so: it proposes regulatory action and more rigorous research—exactly the responses the critique would motivate. Choices A and D would continue in a positive, supportive vein that is inconsistent with the cautionary turn the passage has taken. They would be logical continuations of an earlier, more optimistic paragraph, but not of this critical one. Choice C would be a return to background information that belongs earlier in a passage, not after a critique has been leveled. The structure of the passage at this point demands a response to the problem just identified, and Choice B provides that response.

image.alt: A two-panel layout: the top panel shows the clock passage with its thesis and contrast structure mapped; the bottom panel shows the microbiome passage with an arrow indicating the logical direction of continuation.
image.caption: SAT structure questions test the ability to see the passage as a structured argument, with each paragraph serving a specific function within the overall organization

summary:
Understanding text structure is the skill of recognizing the organizational framework that governs how ideas are sequenced and related in a passage. The six most common structures on the SAT—Thesis-Evidence, Problem-Solution, Cause-Effect, Compare-Contrast, Chronological, and Question-Answer/Hypothesis-Testing—each establish distinct logical relationships and are identifiable by characteristic signal words and organizational patterns. A passage may combine multiple structures, but the skilled reader identifies the dominant structure and uses it as a framework for understanding the passage as a whole.

The process for identifying structure involves asking, 'What large-scale logical move does this passage make from beginning to end?' The answer to this question reveals whether the passage moves from claim to evidence, problem to solution, cause to effect, comparison to conclusion, or question to answer. Surface features—such as temporal markers—can be misleading if they are not the true organizing principle; the reader must look past them to the underlying logical architecture.

SAT structure questions test multiple levels of structural awareness. Overall organization questions ask about the passage's primary structure. Paragraph function questions ask what a specific section contributes to the whole—providing evidence, establishing context, presenting a counterargument, drawing a conclusion. Transition questions ask about the logical relationship signaled by words like 'however,' 'furthermore,' and 'consequently.' Continuation questions ask what would logically follow based on the structural logic established. All of these questions are answered by understanding how the passage is built.

Constructing a structural map of the passage—a brief mental outline of what each paragraph does and how it connects to the thesis—is the most powerful tool for answering structure questions. This map provides the framework within which all other questions about the passage can be answered. The student who knows where the thesis is, where the evidence is, where the counterargument is addressed, and where the conclusion begins can locate information efficiently and understand the function of each part within the whole. Text structure is thus not just one more question type to master; it is the organizing intelligence that makes all other question types more manageable.
